analogy: Think of "Power Sharing" through the chapter's own opening images: P o w e r - s h a r i n g 1 Chapter I Power-sharing Overview With this chapter, we resume the tour of democracy that we started last year. We noted last year that in a democracy all power does not rest... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 1 Power is shared among different, 2 Power can be shared among, 3 Power may also be shared, 4 Power sharing arrangements. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Power Sharing is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that p o w e r - s h a r i n g 1 Chapter I Power-sharing Overview With this chapter, we resume the tour of democracy that we started last year.

The text explains that we noted last year that in a democracy all power does not rest with any one organ of the government.

The text explains that an intelligent sharing of power among legislature, executive and judiciary is very important to the design of a democracy.

The text explains that in this and the next two chapters, we carry this idea of power-sharing forward.

The text explains that we start with two stories from Belgium and Sri Lanka.

The text explains that both these stories are about how democracies handle demands for power-sharing.

The text explains that the stories yield some general conclusions about the need for power-sharing in democracy.

The text explains that this allows us to discuss various forms of power-sharing that will be taken up in the following two chapters.

The text explains that 2 D e m o c r a t i c P o l i t i c s Belgium and Sri Lanka I have a simple equation in mind.

The text explains that sharing power = dividing power = weakening the country.

The text explains that ethnic: A social division based on shared culture.

The text explains that people belonging to the same ethnic group believe in their common descent because of similarities of physical type or of culture or both.

The text explains that they need not always have the same religion or nationality.

The text explains that belgium is a small country in Europe, smaller in area than the state of Haryana.

The text explains that it has borders with France, the Netherlands, Germany and Luxembourg.

The text explains that it has a population of a little over one crore, about half the population of Haryana.

The text explains that the leaders of the Sinhala community sought to secure dominance over government by virtue of their majority.

The text explains that as a result, the democratically elected government adopted a series of majoritarian measures to establish Sinhala supremacy.

The text explains that the governments followed preferential policies that favoured Sinhala applicants for university positions and government jobs.

The text explains that a new constitution stipulated that the state shall protect and foster Buddhism.

The text explains that all these government measures, coming one after the other, gradually increased the feeling of alienation among the Sri Lankan Tamils.

The text explains that they felt that the constitution and government policies denied them equal political rights, discriminated against them in getting jobs and other opportunities and ignored their interests.

The text explains that i am glad our Constitution does not say which minister will come from which community.

The text explains that between 1970 and 1993, they amended their constitution four times so as to work out an arrangement that would enable everyone to live together within the same country.

The text explains that here are some of the elements of the Belgian model:  Constitution prescribes that the number of Dutch and French-speaking ministers shall be equal in the central government.

The text explains that thus, no single community can make decisions unilaterally.  Many powers of the Central Government have been given to State Governments of the two regions of the country.

The text explains that the State Governments are not subordinate to the Central Government.  Brusselshasaseparategovernment in which both the communities have equal representation.

The text explains that you will notice that place names and directions in two languages – French and Dutch. © Wikipedia

The text explains that yet, they dealt with the question of power sharing differently.

The text explains that european Parliament in Brussels, Belgium accepted equal representation in the Central Government.

The text explains that apart from the Central and the State Government, there is a third kind of government.

The text explains that this ‘community government’ is elected by people belonging to one language community – Dutch, French and German-speaking – no matter where they live.

The text explains that this government has the power regarding cultural, educational and language-related issues.

The text explains that if the parents of Annette and Selvi were to approach respective governments to realise the desire of the child who is more likely to succeed?

The text explains that thus, two different sets of reasons can be given in favour of power sharing.

The text explains that there is a second, deeper reason why power sharing is good for democracies.

The text explains that people have a right to be consulted on how they are to be governed.

The text explains that a legitimate government is one where citizens, through participation, acquire a stake in the system.

The text explains that let us call the first set of reasons prudential and the second moral.

The text explains that while prudential reasons stress that power sharing will bring out better outcomes, moral reasons emphasise the very act of power sharing as valuable.

The text explains that the cartoon at the left refers to the problems of running the Germany’s grand coalition government that includes the two major parties of the country, namely the Christian Democratic Union and the Social Democratic Party.

The text explains that they had to form a coalition government because neither of them got clear majority of seats on their own in the 2005 elections.

The text explains that they take divergent positions on several policy matters, but still jointly run the government.

The text explains that but under the present system, the top position is out of his reach.

The text explains that his elders, who have seen the bloodshed of the civil war, tell him that the present system is the best guarantee for peace…” The story was not finished, but they had reached the TV tower where they stopped every day.

The text explains that vetal wrapped up quickly and posed his customary question to Vikram: “If you had the power to rewrite the rules in Lebanon, what would you do?

The text explains that 1 Power is shared among different organs of government, such as the legislature, executive and judiciary.

The text explains that let us call this horizontal distribution of power because it allows different organs of government placed at the same level to exercise different powers.

The text explains that different arguments are usually put forth in favour of and against power sharing.

The text explains that identify those which are in favour of power sharing and select the answer using the codes given below?

The text explains that power sharing: A. reduces conflict among different communities B. decreases the possibility of arbitrariness C. delays decision making process D. accommodates diversities E. increases instability and divisiveness F. promotes people’s participation in government G. undermines the unity of a country Exercises (a) A B D F (b) A C E F (c) A B D G (d) B C D G

The text explains that consider the following two statements on power sharing and select the answer using the codes given below: A.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Democratic Politics II, Chapter 1: Power Sharing

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1 Power is shared among different and 2 Power can be shared among as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Power Sharing; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Power Sharing through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1 Power is shared among different and 2 Power can be shared among to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
